steps.title: Compare the genre claims
steps.content: Text 1 says The Known World is too rich and complicated to be described as historical fiction because that label suggests a shallow re-creation of the past. Text 2 directly challenges that assumption. It says historical fiction can explore profound themes and complex characters, so Text 2 would defend the genre rather than reject the label.

steps.title: Reject misread responses
steps.content:
• Choice A — By arguing that Jones displays a thorough knowledge of historical details in The Known World.: Text 2 rejects the idea that historical fiction is mainly a display of period details, so this response would repeat the shallow view Text 2 criticizes.
  • Choice B — By claiming that the author of Text 1 has underestimated the richness and complexity of The Known World.: Text 1 already praises the novel as richer and more complicated than mere historical reconstruction.
  • Choice C — By conceding that the genre of historical fiction contains many works that are less sophisticated than The Known World is.: Text 2 broadly defends historical fiction and does not concede that the genre is generally unsophisticated.

steps.title: Choose the genre defense
steps.content: Choice D says dissatisfaction with calling The Known World historical fiction reflects a misunderstanding of the genre. That matches Text 2's point: critics wrongly treat historical fiction as shallow, when it can be used for meaningful stories, profound themes, complex characters, and creative freedom. The answer is D.
